ocr_text:
Japan Seeking Way to Evolve 2-Party System
Political candidates doggedly compete for votes across Japan in anticipation of October 20 election in which new electoral districts and methods of voting will be used for first time as part of effort to lead Japan in direction of adversarial two-party system; some experts predict any change will be very gradual and will take several elections, while others doubt Japan will end up with anything looking much like Western two-party system; say single party is likely to dominate for indefinite future in consensus society, where issues are not much debated, and where legislators want to be ruling party to dispense pork-barrel projects; photo (M)

Few Tears for the Death of the Network Convention
Cutback on network television coverage of future Presidential conventions, made likely by overly managed 1996 conventions, discussed; diminished importance of conventions and of prime-time television noted (M)

Haitians Serve Politics At Festival
Haitian groups plan to use West Indian Day Parade, Crown Heights, Brooklyn, to call attention to new Federal welfare reform law that cuts off some government benefits to legal immigrants; plan runs counter to parade's tradition as celebratory, not political, event (S)û

For the Great White Way, More Glitz
Numerous signs in Times Square area are gaudier than ever as new technology adds glitz to area New York City seeks to preserve as entertainment district; advertisers are eager to pay stiff prices to have corporate images or products on display in area, visited by some 20 million tourists a year; photo (M)
Asians Join Scramble To Build Regional Jet

Bosnian Leader Hails Islam at Election Rallies
Bosnian Pres Alija Izetbegovic hails Islam at rally that includes Koranic prayers, music and slogans; photo; his governing party has shown little tolerance toward dissenters during election campaign, which adds to questions about viability of multiethnic government envisioned in Dayton peace accords; one foreign diplomat suggests that Croats and Serbs understand better than West that Izetvegovic's goal really is to establish Muslim state (S)

Asians Join Scramble To Build Regional Jet
At least three rival groups based in Asia aim to begin assembling regional jetliners, but analysts warn that the market is already crowded and growth potential limited..They say that the competition for sales in the category of jets that seat between 9

Keep Retaliation Limited, Allies Urge Clinton
Key allies are quietly urging the Clinton administration to limit any retaliation against Iraq to a sharply contained, well-defined blow, probably in the form of an air strike, British and French officials said Monday..In their statements, European gov

NEW YORKERS & CO.
Women's sportswear company Kenar expects to open its flagship store on Madison Avenue next month (S)

Nursery Plan or Sand Mining?
Proposal by Joseph Marando Nurseries Inc of Manorville, NY, to level hill to expand nursery draws lawsuit by Long Island Pine Barrens Society, which charges nursery's real purpose is lucrative sand mining, not business expansion; in addition, plan has divided five voting members of Long Island Pine Barrens Commission, which was created by state in 1995 to protect 100,000-acre area; in August, three commissioners voted without comment to allow nursery to excavate kane (hill formed by retreating glacier); vote reinforced conditional approval from Town of Brookhaven, which has jurisdiction over site; society official says nursery's intention to mine became obvious after it rejected an alternative proposal from County Planning Department to terrace rather than remove the material; says there is no reason to remove the hill except for sand mining; nursery plan seen major test of how commission will carry out its stewardship over the pines; photo (M)

Brahms and Schubert, All Day, All the Time
James R Oestreich preview of upcoming concert season notes Schubert and Brahms will be richly represented in concert; photos of Renee Fleming and Claudio Abbado (S)

India Counts on Vote to Blunt Kashmir Insurgency
Kashmir will hold election beginning on Sept 7 in what India hopes will be major step toward ending Pakistani-backed Muslim insurgency there; election will be held over four days; map; photo (M)

Sampras Still Striving To Fulfill Expectations
Harvey Araton Sports of The Times article on role of Dr Pete Fischer in helping tennis player Pete Sampras develop his game; photo (M)

Shouting's Over. Now, Bosnians Vote.
Crowds gather in Banja Luka, Bosnia, for flurry of political rallies as country prepares to vote Sept 14 to fill regional and national offices created under Dayton peace plan; photo (M)

Party Leader Tests Gears in Brooklyn Machine
Assemblyman Clarence Norman Jr discusses election of his candidate, Justice Michael H Feinberg, in Demoratic primary for Brooklyn surrogate and how it defines his stewardship as leader of Democratic Party in Brooklyn; photo (M)

Red Sox Enjoying A Rebirth
Boston Red Sox baseball team, 16 games out of first place and 13 games under .500 at beginning of July, has turned itself around, going 33-14 since then and is now back in pennant race; photo of game in which Boston defeated New York Yankees in Bronx (M)

Making It In New York, New York
Claire Smith, in Sports of The Times column, comments on excitement building among baseball fans in New York City following Yankees' winning division championship; prospect of Yankees appearing in World Series discussed; Darryl Strawberry comments; photo (M)

Cowgirls Get Inequality Blues
Women's rodeo is growing, and some women plan to compete against men; there are four times as many men's rodeos as women's, and women's rodeos attract smaller crowds and cannot offer the same kind of prize money; Tammy Kelly, a bull rider, says she wants the bigger earnings of the men's circuit; photo (M)

Japanese Voters, Too, Get a Big Tax-Cut Promise
Japanese opposition leader Ichiro Ozawa, trying to win support in Oct 20 general elections, calls for big tax cuts (M)

Braves Slug Away at Nomo To Finish Sweep of Dodgers
Atlanta Braves defeat Los Angeles Dodgers, 5-2, and win National League Division Series three games to none (M)

Mechanic of Japan's Machine Politics Stalls a Bit
Ichiro Ozawa, opposition leader who is legendary mechanic of Japan's political machines, faces poor poll results going into general elections; his difficulties underscore weakness of opposition politics in Japan; Americans see Ozawa as fresh force for change, but to many Japanese he epitomizes old machine politics they are trying to put behind them; even when he makes points that resonate with the public, people criticize him as an opportunist; photo (M)

Crucial Midwest States Lean Toward Clinton
Crucial Midwestern states are seen tipping markedly, if not yet decisively, in Pres Clinton's direction; in states where Democratic Presidential nominees usually win without much difficulty, like Minnesota, Clinton seems unbeatable; in battleground states, like Missouri and Ohio, he is running strongly; even in Indiana, historically Republicans' most reliable regional redoubt, recent poll shows Clinton with narrow advantage; chart (L)

Dole Shows High Spirits Across Michigan
Bob Dole campaigns across Michigan; predicts victory, despite polls, which show him falling increasingly behind; photo (M)

In This Campaign, the Most Telling Signs in Oregon Say 'Help Wanted'
Article in series of election profiles focuses on Oregon, where voters are pleased with economy and Pres Clinton has wide lead over Bob Dole; photo (M)

Algeria Holding Talks Without the Opposition
Major Algerian opposition parties boycott or are barred form national conference aimed at ending an era of violence and political paralysis; Pres Liamine Zeroual declares 'The destabilization has failed' (S)

Scores of House Candidates Dancing the Texas Shuffle
Texas has had plenty of raucous free-for-alls in its electoral history, but it would be hard to match this year's House races, with contests in 13 of 30 districts thrown into turmoil by redistricting fracas; commotion began with US Supreme Court's June ruling of unconstitutional racial gerrymandering, followed by appeals court's redrawing of those three districts and ten adjacent ones; photo of Ken Bentsen, nephew of former Sen Lloyd Bentsen, who represents one of districts (S)

Jets' New Casualty: Graham
New York Jet wide receiver Jeff Graham undergoes surgery to repair cartilege damage and will be out from three to four weeks; ten Jet starters have been sidelined this year; Graham photo (M)

Thurmond's Robust Legend Shields Him at 93
US Sen Strom Thurmond, running for eighth term at age 93, is revered and loved by vast majority of South Carolinians, despite misgivings about his age; has built reservoir of good will throughout career, with gestures large and small; polls show him 10 percentage points ahead of Democratic challenger, Elliott S Close, who has made Thurmond's age and competence election issue; photo (M)

In Atlanta Games, Yanks Sweep the Gold
New York Yankees defeat Atlanta Braves, 1-0, Atlanta-Fulton County Stadium, to take 3-2 lead in World Series; Yankees' Andy Pettitte pitches stunning five-hitter over eight and third innings; photos (L)

Error Fans Fears of Chemical Cleanup at Utah Plant
Small leak of sarin nerve gas inside weapons-destruction factory in Tooele, Utah, raises questions about its safety; plant managers say that although $650 million factory has state-of-the-art technology, the leak was probably caused by something as simple as leaky gaskets; plant was closed for five days; plant officials say leak of gas posed no health risk to workers or to public; photo; map (M)
N.F.C. Scouting Reports

Scott and Zelda: Their Style Lives
Eleanor Lanahan article on 100th anniversary of birth of her grandfather, F Scott Fitzgerald, discusses influence of Fitzgerald and her grandmother, Zelda, on 20th century popular culture; photos (M)
A.F.C Scouting Reports

Dole Camp Says Consider Nader for Debate
Bob Dole's campaign manager, Scott Reed, says Ralph Nader, nominee of Green Party, should be considered as possible participant in any Presidential debates; Ross Perot's candidacy is generally viewed as taking votes away from Dole; Nader could possibly draw votes from Clinton in states where Green Party is strong, like California (S)

Giants Start the Season With a Moral Victory
Buffalo Bills defeat New York Giants, 23-20, in opening game of season; photos (S)

AT&T Assails a Study by Economist
AT&T mounts major counterattack against book by economist Paul W MacAvoy which contends that long-distance telephone market has functioned since 1990 as oligarchy, with AT&T setting rates which give carriers profit margins of more than 60 percent; AT&T reportedly pressured co-publisher, American Enterprise Institute, to also put out study countering MacAvoy's arguments; MacAvoy himself has had ties to Baby Bells, and critics note he did not include AT&T's vast ad expenses in calculating costs; MacAvoy and AT&T spokesman Peter Pitsch comment; photo (M)

Perot Begins Campaign, on TV, With Charts
Ross Perot begins Presidential campaign with folksy television 'infomercial' calling for privatization of Social Security, reorganization of Medicare and end to 'one-sided trade deals'; displays trademark charts in first of series of broadcasts financed largely with $29 million in Federal election capaign funds (S)

Internet Future At I.B.M. Looks Oddly Familiar
IBM strategy for Internet is expected to concentrate on behind-the-scenes computer technology, in line with mainstay business; division chief Irving Wladawsky-Berger is expected to tell analysts that IBM has no designs on Internet browser wars that get public attention, believing that real money will be made as thousands of companies institute new computer networks (S)

Western Butterfly Shifting North as Global Climate Warms
Dr Camille Parmesan, California biologist, comes up with what scientists say is clearest evidence so far that global warming is forcing living species to move toward poles and to higher altitudes; experts have long predicted that many organisms would shift their ranges in response to climatic change; Parmesan, in study that is said to be first of its kind to cover a species' full geographic range, finds that range of western butterfly, the Edith's checkerspot, has shifted northward by about 100 miles; photo (M)

Cigar Again Facing A Two-Pronged Assault
Preview of Breeders Cup races in Toronto; Bill Mott, trainer of Cigar, comments (M)

Q & A / Thomas Mann : With Dole in Doldrums, the Real Race Is for Congress
Thomas Mann is director of governmental studies at the Brookings Institution in Washington and a former executive director of the American Political Science Association. He spoke about the coming U.S. elections with Brian Knowlton of the International He

Eyeless in Hebron
Thomas L Friedman Op-Ed column says if Israelis and Palestinians fail to come to agreement on power-sharing in Hebron, peace process will go into nosedive, with all the dangerous consequences it entails; says devil in Hebron is not in details, but in failure of both sides to see big picture (M)

Romanians Vote Today, But Change Isn't Likely
Pres Ion Iliescu is expected to win re-election in Romania; his governing party trails coalition of market reform proponents in polls; Iliescu's hold on power is puzzling in face of sharp drop in living standards (M)

SYDNEY NOTEBOOK : Group Plans for Plenty of Rooms With a View
Hongkong & Shanghai Hotels Ltd., also known as the Peninsula Group, is likely to win approval for a new hotel on one of Sydney's most coveted sites — Bennelong Point. The site overlooks the Sydney Opera House and Sydney Harbor Bridge..In June
